title: How to Ask for Academic Accommodations Without Feeling Like an Imposter
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have a diagnosis. You have documentation. You know you would benefit from extended time on exams, a quiet testing environment, or permission to record lectures. But when you think about actually requesting these accommodations, something stops you. A voice in your head says you do not really need them. Other people have it worse. You have managed so far without help. Asking would be taking advantage. You would be getting something you do not deserve. This voice is wrong. Accommodations are not a privilege for people who are struggling more visibly than you. They are a legal right designed to level the playing field. Here is why you deserve them and how to request them without the guilt.

Why You Feel Like an Imposter

The feeling that you are not disabled enough for accommodations is common among students with ADHD. Part of the reason is that ADHD is invisible. You look fine. You can function well in some contexts. You have developed coping strategies that mask your difficulties. From the outside, you appear to be managing. The gap between how you appear and how you struggle creates a cognitive dissonance. If other people cannot see the problem, maybe the problem is not real.

Another reason is that many students with ADHD have internalized a lifetime of being told they are not trying hard enough. Teachers, parents, and peers have attributed your difficulties to laziness or lack of effort. After years of hearing that message, you start to believe it. Accommodations feel like a confirmation of the accusation. If you need extra time, it must mean you really are not trying hard enough. This is not true. Accommodations are not a substitute for effort. They are a recognition that your effort has been applied against an unfair disadvantage.

There is also the comparison trap. You know someone whose ADHD is more severe than yours. They cannot function without medication. They failed courses before getting accommodations. You have not failed. You are getting by. But getting by is not the same as performing at the level of your ability. Accommodations exist to close the gap between your performance and your potential, not just to prevent failure. You do not need to hit rock bottom before you are allowed to ask for help.

What Accommodations Actually Do

Accommodations do not give you an advantage. They remove barriers that neurotypical students do not face. Extended time on exams does not make the material easier. It compensates for the slower processing speed and distractibility that are core features of ADHD. A quiet testing environment does not make you smarter. It removes the sensory distractions that disproportionately affect your ability to concentrate. Permission to record lectures does not mean you do not have to pay attention. It means you can review material you may have missed when your attention momentarily drifted.

Think of accommodations as glasses. A student who needs glasses is not getting an unfair advantage by wearing them. They are being brought to the same baseline that students with normal vision have by default. Your accommodations are the same. They bring you to the baseline. They do not elevate you above it.

Accommodations also recognize that ADHD is a performance variability disorder. You do not have the same level of access to your abilities every day. On a good day, you might perform close to your potential without accommodations. On a bad day, the gap is larger. Accommodations protect you against the bad days. They ensure that your grade reflects your knowledge and ability, not the random fluctuation of your symptoms.

How to Make the Request

The process starts with your university's disability services office, which may be called accessibility services, student disability resources, or a similar name. You will need documentation of your diagnosis. This usually means a letter from your treating physician or psychologist that states your diagnosis, describes the functional limitations it causes, and recommends specific accommodations. The disability services office will review your documentation and determine which accommodations are appropriate. You will then receive a letter to share with your professors.

When you share the letter with a professor, you do not need to disclose your diagnosis. The letter states what accommodations you are entitled to, not why. Most professors will simply acknowledge it and ask if there is anything you need to arrange. You can say as much or as little as you want about your ADHD. Some students prefer to be open. Others prefer privacy. Both are valid.

If a professor pushes back or seems skeptical, remember that accommodations are not a favor they are granting. They are a legal requirement under the Americans with Disabilities Act and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act. You do not need to convince them you deserve it. The disability services office has already made that determination. If you encounter resistance, refer the professor to the disability services office. You do not have to advocate for yourself alone.

The Bottom Line

Requesting academic accommodations for ADHD is not an admission of failure or a request for unfair advantage. It is an acknowledgment that you have a neurological condition that affects your ability to demonstrate your knowledge under standard conditions. The voice telling you that you do not deserve help is the internalized echo of people who did not understand your brain. You do not need to listen to it. Contact your university's disability services office. Provide your documentation. Share your accommodation letter with your professors. Use the accommodations. They exist because students like you need them. You are one of those students.
